topic: making pelmeni for friends — Babushka would approve

turns.content: so I'm making pelmeni for like twelve people tomorrow night and I just realized I don't have enough freezer space for all of them. classic
Twelve people is a lot of pelmeni! How many are you planning per person? And how much freezer space are we actually working with?

minimum fifteen per person, maybe twenty for the guys who eat like animals. so let's say... two hundred pelmeni? give or take? and my freezer is already full of stocks and sauces I brought home from work. it's a disaster in there

Two hundred pelmeni is a serious production run. For the freezer issue, you could do them in batches — freeze the first batch on sheet pans lined with parchment, then once they're firm, stack them into bags to free up the tray space for the next round. That compresses the footprint a lot. Or are you planning to cook them all fresh?

non non, I want them fresh. I'm folding them tomorrow afternoon and cooking them right before everyone arrives. the problem is I need them all made and sitting somewhere before they go into the pot, and there's only so much counter space. I'll probably use every sheet pan I own plus the dining table covered in parchment

The dining table as overflow workspace is smart. If you flour the parchment well they won't stick. Just make sure the room isn't too warm or the dough gets tacky. Are you doing the folding solo or do you have help?

my friend Anya said she'd come help but knowing Anya she'll fold five, drink three glasses of wine, and then start giving me unsolicited opinions about my love life. so basically I'm solo. but it's fine, I'm fast. Babushka used to do five hundred for New Year's and she was seventy-three years old so I have no excuse
Five hundred at seventy-three is legendary. What filling are you going with? Classic pork and beef, or are you doing something different?

classic. pork and beef with onion, salt, pepper, tiny bit of garlic. nothing fancy. when it comes to pelmeni I don't mess around with "elevated" versions or whatever. the whole point is that it's simple and perfect. you boil them, you put sour cream and dill on top, maybe a little vinegar, and you eat thirty of them and feel like everything is okay in the world
There's something to be said for leaving a dish exactly as it's meant to be. No need to deconstruct or foam anything. What about the dough — do you have a specific ratio you stick to?

flour, egg, water, salt. I do it by feel mostly. the dough should be smooth and elastic, not too sticky, not too dry. I roll it thin — thinner than most people do, actually. I hate thick doughy pelmeni. the wrapper should almost disappear when you bite in, just a little resistance and then the meat and the juice. if the dough is the main event you've done it wrong
Thin wrapper, juicy filling — that's the mark of someone who knows what they're doing. The juice is key. Do you add any liquid to the filling, or does it come from the fat ratio in the meat?

fat ratio. you need enough fat in the pork or it's dry and sad. I do about 70/30 on the pork side. some people add a splash of ice water to the filling which I think is fine but I don't bother. if your meat is good and your seal is tight the juice stays in. putain, now I'm hungry and it's midnight. I should go prep the filling tonight so tomorrow it's just roll and fold

That's a good call — getting the filling done tonight means tomorrow is pure assembly. Mix it, cover it, fridge it. The flavours will meld overnight anyway. And make sure Anya brings more wine since she won't be useful for the folding.

hah she always brings wine, that's never the problem. ok I'm going to go grind this meat and channel my inner Babushka. if the pelmeni are good tomorrow I'll be happy. if they're not I'll blame Anya for distracting me. bonne nuit
Bonne nuit. Two hundred pelmeni, thin wrappers, good meat, sour cream and dill. Your guests are lucky. Go get that filling done and get some sleep — you've got a long afternoon of folding ahead.
